originalTitle: 1996: Dolly the sheep, the first mammal cloned from an adult cell, is born

A1.title: A special sheep is born
A1.text: It is July 5, 1996. A little sheep is born in Scotland. Her name is Dolly. She looks like a normal sheep. But she is very special. Scientists made her in a laboratory. She is a copy of another sheep. A copy like this is called a clone. Dolly is the first animal of this kind. The scientists keep her a secret for months. Then the whole world learns about her. Everyone talks about Dolly, the famous sheep.

vocabulary.definition: a farm animal with warm wool
when a baby comes into the world
a thing that is the same as another thing
something you do not tell people

A2.title: Dolly, the cloned sheep
A2.text: On July 5, 1996, a lamb was born at a research institute near Edinburgh, in Scotland. Her name was Dolly, and she became the most famous sheep in history. Dolly was a clone: scientists made her from one single cell of an adult sheep. She was an exact copy of that sheep. Before Dolly, many people thought this was impossible with animals like sheep. The scientists kept her birth secret for seven months. When they told the world in February 1997, the news went around the planet. Some people were excited about this new science. Others were worried and asked: what will people clone next? Dolly lived for six years and had six lambs.

vocabulary.definition: careful study to learn new things
an exact living copy of an animal or plant
the smallest living part of a body
feeling afraid that something bad can happen

B1.title: The day Dolly the sheep was born
B1.text: On July 5, 1996, a lamb called Dolly was born at the Roslin Institute near Edinburgh, Scotland — and science changed forever. Dolly was the first mammal ever cloned from a cell of an adult animal. Scientists took a single cell from a six-year-old sheep, placed its genetic material inside an empty egg, and grew it into a lamb. Dolly was, in effect, an exact genetic copy of the older sheep. Until that day, most experts believed such a thing was impossible: adult cells, they thought, could never start life again from the beginning. The team kept Dolly secret for seven months while they checked their results. When the news finally came out in February 1997, it caused a worldwide sensation. Governments began writing rules about cloning, newspapers asked whether humans would be next, and Dolly — named after the singer Dolly Parton — became a celebrity. She lived for six years, had six lambs, and can still be seen today, preserved at the National Museum of Scotland.

vocabulary.definition: an animal whose babies drink their mother's milk
connected with the information inside cells that parents pass to children
an event that causes great excitement
a very famous person or animal
kept in good condition for a long time

B2.title: Dolly: the clone that shook science
B2.text: When a lamb was born at the Roslin Institute near Edinburgh on July 5, 1996, nothing about her appearance suggested a revolution. Yet Dolly was unlike any animal born before her: she was the first mammal cloned from an adult cell. The technique behind her birth, known as nuclear transfer, involved taking the nucleus of a cell from a six-year-old ewe's mammary gland and inserting it into an egg cell whose own nucleus had been removed. The reconstructed egg was then implanted into a surrogate mother. The result was a healthy lamb genetically identical to the ewe that had provided the cell — proof that a specialized adult cell could be persuaded to start life over again, something most biologists had
